FOREWORD
Few changes were made in the School Laws by the General
Assembly of 1910. However ,in compliancewith Section4393 and
Section 4395 of the Statutes, we submit this revision. Decisions
of the Court of Appeals, opinionsof the Attorney General, and
Rulings of the Department, bearing on the interpretationof the
School Laws , willbe found in Chapter 23 .
ELLSWORTH REGENSTEIN ,
Superintendent of Public Instruction.

It outlines the process for the Auditor of Public Accounts to distribute funds to County Superintendents and Treasurers of School Boards based on successive warrants from the Superintendent of Public Instruction. It also includes provisions for proportionate distribution if the treasury is insufficient and mandates that no subdistrict be omitted or excluded from participation. Additionally, it establishes the State Board of Education, composed of the Superintendent of Public Instruction, Secretary of State, and Attorney-General, and defines its corporate name.

classification.title: Common School Laws of Kentucky
classification.summary: This section of the Common School Laws of Kentucky outlines the eligibility requirements and examination process for county superintendents. It details the qualifications needed, including state diplomas or certificates, and the procedures for conducting examinations by a special county board composed of the county judge, county clerk, and a competent person selected by them. The process involves sealed questions prepared by the State Board of Examiners, secure handling of these questions, and forwarding of examination answers and fees to the State Board for review and certificate issuance. The statute also specifies that the obtained certificate does not entitle the holder to teach in common schools.

classification.title: Common School Laws 1910
classification.summary: This section of the 1910 Common School Laws of Kentucky outlines eligibility requirements and election procedures for county superintendents. Notably, it states that in counties with a city of the first class maintaining a separate school system, the county superintendent must be a resident of the county outside of that city. This implies a segregation of school systems and potentially a racial or class-based exclusion from leadership roles within the broader county education system.
classification.reasoning: The statute itself does not explicitly mention race. However, the provision stating that in counties with a city of the first class maintaining a 'system of public schools separate and distinct from the common schools of the county,' the county superintendent must reside outside of such city, strongly suggests a de facto segregation. Historically, such separate school systems in the South were often established along racial lines, with Black students and teachers relegated to inferior facilities. The restriction on who can be a county superintendent in such a context, by requiring them to be from outside the segregated urban school district, implies a mechanism to maintain control or influence over the broader county education system, potentially to the detriment of the segregated urban schools or their constituents. This implicit segregation and the potential for discriminatory application warrant classification as a Jim Crow law. The confidence is moderate because the racial aspect is inferred from historical context rather than explicit text. Human review is needed to confirm the historical context of 'separate and distinct' school systems in Kentucky cities of the first class during this period.

classification.title: Common School Laws 1910
classification.summary: This section of the 1910 Common School Laws outlines the procedures for the distribution and sale of adopted textbooks in Kentucky's common schools. It details how county commissions should appoint agents (merchants or other businesses) to sell books to patrons at a price not exceeding a 15% advance on the net contract price, which also covers transportation costs. The law also provides for the exchange of new books for old ones displaced by new adoptions and allows patrons moving to another county to exchange old books for their monetary value. Furthermore, it mandates that county judges purchase suitable second-hand books from these dealers for indigent pupils at a price no more than 10% above the cost of the second-hand book. A penalty is established for agents or dealers who charge more than the lawful price or refuse to make exchanges.

classification.summary: This section of the Common School Laws of Kentucky from 1910 outlines the financial responsibilities and operational procedures for graded common school districts. It details the role of the treasurer in managing funds from bond sales and annual expenses, requiring them to execute a bond for faithful performance. The county superintendent is responsible for paying the pro rata portion of state and county funds to these districts based on pupil enrollment. The board of trustees is empowered to provide funds for grounds, buildings, and equipment, including the authority to issue bonds for these purposes, subject to constitutional limits.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is classified as not being a Jim Crow law.

It covers the honoring of warrants by the treasurer, the continuation of existing indebtedness and liabilities, appropriation of funds, election and duties of the president and secretary, the establishment and maintenance of school libraries, and the admission of non-resident pupils with tuition fees. It does not contain any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative and financial operations of city school boards in Kentucky in 1910. It covers topics such as financial management, board officer duties, library establishment, and pupil admissions. After a thorough review of the text, there are no mentions of race, nor are there any provisions that suggest differential treatment or segregation based on race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses administrative matters without any racial undertones. The document type is inferred as 'codes' because it appears to be part of a compiled set of laws rather than a single act from a legislative session.

classification.title: Common School Laws 1910
classification.summary: This statute, Section 326, grants cities of the fourth class the power to separate their existing graded free schools into distinct white and colored common schools. Each system would be governed by its own board of trustees and supported by its proportional share of the State School Fund, paid directly by the State Superintendent of Public Instruction.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates the separation of public schools into 'white common school' and 'colored common school' for cities of the fourth class. This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The statute also details the funding mechanism for these separate schools, reinforcing the segregationist structure. Therefore, it is classified as Jim Crow with high confidence. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' because it is presented as part of a compilation of school laws from a specific year (1910) and references acts approved on specific dates (June 28, 1903, and March 18, 1904), which is typical of legislative session laws.

classification.title: Common School Laws 1910
classification.summary: This statute mandates the separation of white and colored common schools within a city. It specifies that taxes raised from white individuals or corporations shall not be used for colored schools, and vice versa. The statute also outlines the process for fixing tax amounts for each racial group's schools and the appointment of separate boards of trustees. It further details the conveyance of school property to the respective racial school boards and allows for elections to abolish either the white or colored graded common school system.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mandates the separation of schools based on race ('white' and 'colored'). It details how funding, property, and governance should be handled separately for each racial group, which is a hallmark of Jim Crow legislation in the context of education. The confidence is high due to the explicit mention of racial categories and the clear establishment of separate systems. The document type is inferred as 'session_laws' from the title 'Common School Laws 1910' and the page numbering suggesting it's part of a legislative compilation.

classification.title: Common School Laws 1910
classification.summary: This section of the 1910 Common School Laws outlines the process for voting on the abolition of either white or colored graded common school systems within a district. It also establishes two State Normal School districts, Eastern and Western Kentucky, with their boundaries to be determined based on white population, and mandates the creation of separate Boards of Regents for each.

This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The establishment of separate normal schools based on racial population further reinforces this classification. classification.title: Board of Regents to Confer Certificates
classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers of the Board of Regents of normal schools to confer educational certificates (Elementary, Intermediate, and Advanced) upon students. It details the requirements for each certificate, their validity periods, and the process for extending the Advanced Certificate for life based on satisfactory teaching experience and good moral character. The State Superintendent of Public Instruction's endorsement is required to validate all certificates and extensions.

classification.title: Common School Laws 1910
classification.summary: This section of the Common School Laws of Kentucky from 1910 outlines the procedures for revoking teaching certificates, the powers of Boards of Regents in normal schools, and the causes and processes for removing school officers and teachers. It details requirements for notifying the State Superintendent of Public Instruction about certificate revocations and for teachers to register with County Superintendents. It also specifies grounds for removal such as incompetency, neglect of duty, or immoral conduct, and mandates a ten-day written notice and an opportunity for defense before removal.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details administrative procedures for the certification and removal of teachers and school officials within Kentucky's common school system. It focuses on the qualifications, revocation processes, and disciplinary actions related to teaching credentials and employment. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, this statute is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is purely administrative and procedural regarding education personnel.

classification.summary: This section of the Common School Laws of Kentucky from 1910 outlines the powers and responsibilities of a board of trustees for an educational institution. It details their authority to appoint and remove officers and teachers, determine salaries, grant degrees, and manage the institution's affairs. Specifically, it states that no preference shall be shown to any religious denomination in the appointment of presidents, professors, or instructors. It also details meeting schedules and the appointment of an executive committee.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the administrative and governance powers of a board of trustees for an educational institution. It focuses on the appointment and removal of staff, salary determination, degree granting, and operational procedures. While it mentions that no preference should be shown to any religious denomination, there is no mention of race or any provisions that would suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is classified as 'other' because the provided text is a snippet from a larger document titled 'Common School Laws 1910.pdf', and it's not explicitly identified as session laws, codes, or other specific legislative document types.

classification.title: Appropriation for State University Buildings and Grounds
classification.summary: This statute appropriates $200,000 for the State University in Lexington, Kentucky. The funds are designated for paying outstanding debts for building erection and equipment, and for the construction and equipment of new facilities including a building for mining engineering, civil engineering, and physics; an addition to the chemical building; an addition to the mechanical and electrical engineering building; and a new dormitory for male students. The board of trustees has discretion over the order of construction and location of improvements, and can purchase additional grounds if necessary. Title to acquired real estate will be held by the Commonwealth for the university's benefit. Contracts for materials, labor, and construction must be let to the lowest and best bidder after advertisement.

classification.title: Common School Laws of Kentucky
classification.summary: This statute establishes a department for the education of colored students in agriculture and the mechanic arts within a normal school. It outlines admission requirements for these students, including age, health, moral character, and a pledge to teach in colored common schools. Tuition is free for eligible colored residents who meet the conditions. The statute also mentions the equitable division of funds from public land sales appropriated by Congress for such institutions.
classification.reasoning: The statute explicitly mentions 'colored students' and establishes a separate educational department for them. This is a clear indicator of racial segregation in education, a hallmark of Jim Crow laws. The statute also requires a pledge from these students to teach in 'colored common schools,' further reinforcing the segregated educational system. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law.

Denominational Schools not Combined With Public Schools-
Under Sections5 and 189 of the Constitution, followedby Section

any common school with any sectarianschool can ever be accom-
plished, and that no State fund must go out to the aid of any such
